title: How Trauma Disrupts the Body's Internal Clock
researcherTopic: structural dissociation, healing the fragmented self
metaDescription: Trauma can disrupt the body's internal clock, leaving you feeling stuck in the past. Learn how to gently recalibrate your nervous system and return to the flow of time.
imageDescription: A soft, golden light illuminates a series of interlocking, transparent gears, some of which are subtly misaligned. The overall feeling is one of gentle mechanics, warmth, and the potential for realignment and healing. The background is a soft, out-of-focus warmth, suggesting a return to harmony.
htmlContent:
Janina Fisher, a pivotal voice in understanding the legacy of trauma, has spent decades mapping the terrain of the fragmented self, revealing how overwhelming experiences don’t just leave psychological scars but fundamentally reorganize our inner architecture. This isn’t merely about bad memories; it’s about a profound disruption that can unmoor us from the natural rhythms of life, including the very cadence of time itself. When the body’s clock is shattered by trauma, we can find ourselves living in a perpetual yesterday, where the past isn’t just a memory but a recurring, visceral reality that dictates the texture of our present moment. The nervous system, in its attempt to keep us safe, can get stuck in a loop, replaying threats that have long since passed, making it feel as though time has folded in on itself, leaving us stranded outside its normal flow.

The Echo of a Disrupted Now

For many who have navigated the labyrinth of complex trauma, the experience of time is not a steady, linear progression but a fractured and unpredictable landscape. It is a phenomenon where the past bleeds into the present with such intensity that the 'now' becomes a ghost, haunted by the echoes of what was. We might find ourselves reacting to a subtle shift in someone's tone or a fleeting expression with a level of activation that feels entirely disproportionate, only to realize later that the reaction was not to the present event but to a deeply ingrained memory held in the body. This is the legacy of a nervous system that has learned to prioritize survival over all else, a system that has become so attuned to threat that it perceives danger even in its absence. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen countless individuals describe this feeling of being perpetually out of sync with their own lives, as if they are watching a film of their experiences rather than living them directly. The body, in its profound and sometimes painful wisdom, remembers the threat so vividly that it refuses to believe the danger has passed, creating a temporal dissonance that can be both disorienting and deeply isolating.

There's a meaningful difference between self-improvement and self-understanding. One adds. The other reveals.

When the Body’s Clock Runs on Survival Time

The human organism is a masterpiece of rhythmic intelligence, a symphony of biological clocks that govern everything from our sleep-wake cycles to our metabolic processes. These internal chronometers are designed to be exquisitely sensitive to the environment, synchronizing our inner world with the outer world’s cycles of light and dark, activity and rest. Trauma, however, acts as a seismic shock to this delicate system, forcing it to abandon its natural cadence and adopt a new, more urgent one: survival time. When the nervous system is chronically activated by threat, it shifts its resources away from the long-term, restorative functions of the body and toward the immediate, life-preserving imperatives of fight, flight, or freeze. This is not a cognitive choice but a biological mandate, a deep, instinctual re-calibration that occurs far below the level of conscious thought. The result is a body that is perpetually running on emergency fuel, a state of being that is both exhausting and unsustainable. We may find ourselves unable to sleep, even when we are bone-tired, or unable to properly digest our food, even when we are hungry. These are not signs of a broken body but of a body that is intelligently, if painfully, adapted to a world that it no longer trusts to be safe. It’s a state that many find themselves in for years, even decades, before they find their way to resources like the Internal Family Systems Institute, which offers a map to understanding these internal conflicts.

Complexity is the ego's favorite hiding place.

This shift into survival time is what Janina Fisher refers to as a key component of structural dissociation, where different parts of the self become compartmentalized in the wake of trauma. One part of us may be trying to live in the present, to go to work, to maintain relationships, to plan for the future, while another part remains frozen in the past, forever scanning the horizon for danger. This internal fragmentation is not a sign of weakness but a testament to the psyche’s incredible capacity to adapt and survive in the face of the unbearable. The challenge, then, is not to force these fragmented parts into a premature and inauthentic unity, but to learn to meet them with a quality of compassionate awareness, to begin the slow and patient work of internal diplomacy. It is about understanding that the part of you that is always on high alert is not trying to sabotage your life but to save it. It is about recognizing that the part of you that feels numb and disconnected is not broken but is wisely conserving energy in the face of overwhelming activation. This is the beginning of the journey back to ourselves, a journey that is less about fixing and more about listening, less about conquering and more about coming home. It is a path that requires us to move beyond the simplistic narratives of recovery and into the much more nuanced and compassionate territory of understanding the architecture of our own survival.

The Unreliable Narrator of Felt Time

One of the most disorienting consequences of a trauma-disrupted internal clock is the profound unreliability of our own felt sense of time. We learn that the clock on the wall and the calendar on our desk are telling one story, while the sensations and activations in our body are telling another, far more urgent one. An afternoon can stretch into an eternity when we are caught in the grip of an emotional flashback, that state where a present-day trigger plunges us back into the emotional reality of a past event without the accompanying visual or auditory memory. Conversely, entire weeks can blur into an indistinct haze of dissociation, a protective mechanism where the mind checks out to escape the unceasing demands of a hypervigilant nervous system. This is not a failure of memory or a lack of discipline; it is the logical outcome of a system that has been rewired to perceive the world through the lens of threat. The body becomes the primary arbiter of our temporal experience, its sensations of dread, panic, or numbness overriding the cognitive mind’s attempts to stay anchored in the present. A client once described this as living in a house where all the clocks are broken, each one showing a different time, and the only way to know what’s real is to listen to the creaks in the floorboards.

Learning to navigate this temporal fragmentation requires a radical shift in our approach to healing. Instead of trying to force the body to conform to the linear time of the outside world, we must learn to listen to its unique and often nonlinear language. This means creating moments of intentional pause, opportunities to check in with the internal landscape of sensation without judgment or agenda. It means recognizing that the sudden wave of exhaustion that washes over you in the middle of the day might not be a sign of laziness but a signal from a younger part of you that is finally feeling safe enough to rest. It is a process of becoming a student of our own nervous system, of learning to read its subtle cues and respond with curiosity rather than criticism. The International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation (ISST-D) provides extensive resources on this very topic, emphasizing the importance of this internal attunement. We begin to understand that healing is not about erasing the past but about expanding our capacity to be present with ourselves, right here, right now, in the midst of whatever temporal weather is moving through us. It is a practice of profound self-compassion, of acknowledging the incredible intelligence that has allowed us to survive, even if it has come at the cost of our connection to the steady rhythm of time.

Not every insight requires action. Some just need to be witnessed.

Re-calibrating the Inner Chronometer

If trauma can throw the body’s internal clock into disarray, then the path of healing must involve a gentle and patient process of re-calibration. This is not a matter of simply willing ourselves back into sync with linear time, but of creating the conditions of safety and presence that allow the nervous system to gradually release its grip on the past. It is a journey that unfolds not in weeks or months, but over the course of a lifetime, a slow and steady turning toward the present moment. We begin by cultivating a different kind of relationship with our own internal experience, one that is rooted in curiosity rather than fear. We learn to notice the subtle shifts in our breath, the tightening in our shoulders, the flutter in our chest, not as problems to be solved, but as communications from a part of ourselves that is asking for our attention. This is the foundational work of somatic inquiry, the practice of listening to the body on its own terms, without imposing our own narratives or agendas upon it. It is a process that can be greatly supported by modalities like EMDR, which helps the brain process traumatic memories that have been stuck in the nervous system, as detailed by organizations like EMDRIA.

This process of re-calibration is often a dance between the past and the present, a delicate negotiation between the part of us that is still living in survival time and the part that is yearning to return to the flow of life. We may find that as we begin to feel safer in the present, the past comes rushing in with a new intensity, as if the body is finally daring to thaw what has been frozen for so long. This is not a sign that we are going backward, but that we are finally developing the capacity to be with what was once unbearable. It is a testament to our own resilience, to the incredible wisdom of a system that knows how to heal itself when given the chance. We learn to create small islands of presence in the midst of the storm, moments where we can anchor ourselves in the sensory reality of the now—the feeling of our feet on the ground, the warmth of the sun on our skin, the sound of our own breath. These moments may be fleeting at first, but with practice, they begin to string together, creating a new kind of temporal landscape, one that is less defined by the echoes of the past and more by the possibilities of the present. It is a journey that asks us to reconsider our relationship with healing itself, to move beyond the idea of a cure and into the much more intimate and ongoing practice of befriending ourselves exactly as we are.

Freedom is not the absence of constraint. It's the capacity to choose your relationship to it.

Returning to the River of Time

The return to a more regulated experience of time is not a destination we arrive at, but a river we learn to flow with again. It is a gradual and often imperceptible process, one that is marked less by dramatic breakthroughs and more by a quiet accumulation of moments of presence. We may one day notice that we are able to enjoy a simple pleasure—a cup of tea, a conversation with a friend, a walk in the woods—without the familiar undercurrent of anxiety that has been our constant companion for so long. We may find that we are able to look toward the future not with a sense of dread, but with a glimmer of curiosity, a willingness to not know what comes next. These are the subtle and profound markers of a nervous system that is beginning to trust the present moment, a body that is slowly learning that the war is over. It is a homecoming that is earned not through striving or effort, but through the patient and persistent practice of doing the work of ourselves, right here, right now, with whatever is true in this moment.

This journey of returning to the river of time is one that is deeply personal and unique to each individual. There is no one-size-fits-all map, no five-step plan that can guarantee our arrival. Instead, we must learn to become our own cartographers, to chart our own course through the wild and sometimes treacherous terrain of our own inner world. We learn to trust the wisdom of our own bodies, to honor the rhythms of our own healing, even when they don't make sense to the outside world. We begin to understand that the goal is not to erase the past, but to integrate it into the larger tapestry of our lives, to allow it to become a source of wisdom and compassion rather than a source of shame and fear. It is a process of reclaiming our own wholeness, of remembering that we are not broken, but simply unfinished. And in that unfinishedness, we find our freedom. We find our way back to the simple and profound truth that we are not separate from the flow of life, but an integral part of its unfolding. A truth that is often explored in depth through a dedicated practice of embodied consciousness, which is a core principle in learning about the nature of embodiment itself.

Silence is not the absence of noise. It's the presence of attention.

This is not the end of something. This is the beginning of a conversation between you and your own nervous system that most people never have the courage to start. The question is not whether the conversation will be difficult. The question is whether you will stay in the room when it gets hard.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so exhausted all the time, even when I get enough sleep?

This is a hallmark of a nervous system running on survival time. When the body is chronically braced for threat, it diverts enormous amounts of energy to fuel this state of hypervigilance, leaving little left for restorative processes like deep sleep and digestion. Even if you are in bed for eight hours, your system may not be entering the deep, restful states it needs to truly recover, creating a profound sense of bone-deep weariness that sleep alone cannot seem to touch.

Is it possible to ever feel normal again after complex trauma?

The goal is not a return to a pre-trauma 'normal,' as that state no longer exists, but an integration of the experience into a new, more resilient wholeness. Healing is less about erasing the past and more about expanding your capacity to be present with all parts of yourself, creating a life that feels authentic and aligned with your values now. It is a journey of becoming, where the scars of the past become integrated into a map of profound self-knowledge and compassion, a process you can explore further in understanding how the body holds onto these experiences.

And so we learn, slowly and with great patience, to stop treating our own experience as a problem to be solved. We begin to see that the fractured sense of time, the hypervigilance, the exhaustion—these are not flaws in our character but the echoes of a profound intelligence that knew how to keep us alive. The journey home is not about fixing what is broken, but about witnessing what is here, now, with a quality of attention that is both fierce and forgiving. It is in this witnessing that the shattered pieces of our own timeline begin to reassemble, not back into what they were, but into something new, something whole, something uniquely and resiliently our own.

You are not a problem to be solved. You are a process to be witnessed.

The real question is what you do with this now.
